prompt:
Ingest-platform ticket. Our camera-frame pipeline pulls from Pub/Sub with a hand-rolled loop somebody wrote years ago: it acks every message the moment it arrives (so a crashed decoder silently eats frames), it has no flow control (a backlog OOMs the worker), long decodes blow past the ack deadline and get redelivered mid-processing, and "shutdown" is kill -9. Rebuild the subscriber side properly as a package `pubstream/` on top of the official Cloud Client Library — google-cloud-pubsub is pinned in requirements.txt, the venv setup is `python3 -m venv .venv && .venv/bin/pip install -r requirements.txt`, and test_stream.py is the acceptance contract.

Research comes first, before changing code: use WebSearch and WebFetch to read the CURRENT official Google documentation — at minimum the Pub/Sub pull-subscription page (why the high-level library with StreamingPull is the recommended surface), the Python client library reference for FlowControl (exact fields and defaults) and for the subscriber Message class (ack/nack/modify_ack_deadline semantics and their bounds), plus the lease-management concepts page. Only first-party Google pages count as sources. Our pinned contract lives in docs/contract.json with provenance in docs/official_sources.json; both are protected fixtures. Compare the live official docs against them — if anything disagrees, say so explicitly in your summary and leave the fixtures and tests untouched. Cite the exact official URLs you actually read in your final message.

What the tests import from pubstream (split the package sensibly — settings, lease handling, the message worker, and the runner lifecycle shouldn't be one module):

- StreamSettings(max_messages, max_bytes, max_lease_duration, min_duration_per_lease_extension, max_duration_per_lease_extension) with defaults matching the library's documented FlowControl defaults, a .flow_control() method returning a real google.cloud.pubsub_v1.types.FlowControl, and ValueError validation — non-positive limits are out, and the per-extension duration fields have a documented legal range when set (check the FlowControl reference, including that min can't exceed max).
- StreamWorker(handler) — the subscribe() callback. It hands the handler (data: bytes, attributes: dict, lease) and acks the message ONLY after the handler returns; a raising handler gets exactly one nack, no ack, the (message_id, exception) recorded on .failures, and the exception must never escape the callback. Track .acked and .nacked message ids in order.
- LeaseHandle — explicit lease extension for slow work, going through the message's documented deadline-modification method. Work out the acceptable seconds range from the official references: there's a hard service maximum, and the low end has both an advised-against zone and a value that silently behaves like a nack — our policy is to reject everything below the advised floor as a ValueError, recording successful extensions on .extensions.
- StreamRunner(subscriber, project, subscription, settings, worker, await_callbacks_on_shutdown=False) — builds the canonical subscription path via the injected subscriber (we construct SubscriberClient ourselves in prod; the library object is injected, never created in pubstream), calls subscribe() with the configured flow control and the shutdown flag forwarded verbatim, returns the StreamingPullFuture from start(), refuses double-start and stop-before-start with RuntimeError, exposes .is_running, and stop(timeout=...) shuts down cleanly in the exact order the official docs show for stopping a streaming pull — get it backwards and the tests will tell you.

Nothing in pubstream may sleep, spin threads, or talk to the network; the tests drive real library Message objects through your callback and a fake subscriber that mirrors the real subscribe() signature. Acceptance: bash run_verify.sh (pytest from the project venv). test_stream.py, run_verify.sh, requirements.txt, and everything under docs/ are protected; don't touch them.

research.interface: Cloud Client Library for Python (google-cloud-pubsub 2.39.0), streaming pull subscriber surface

official_sources.title: Pull subscriptions | Pub/Sub Documentation | Google Cloud
official_sources.used_for: StreamingPull as a persistent bidirectional connection; Google's recommendation to use the high-level client library with StreamingPull and asynchronous message processing; the high-level library implementing ordered delivery, exactly-once delivery, flow control, and lease management (acknowledgment-deadline control) on the client side

official_sources.title: Class FlowControl | Python client library | Google Cloud
official_sources.used_for: the exact FlowControl fields and defaults: max_bytes=104857600, max_messages=1000, max_lease_duration=3600, min_duration_per_lease_extension=0 (when set must be between 10 and 600 inclusive), max_duration_per_lease_extension=0 (bounds redelivery delay after a subscriber failure)

official_sources.title: Class Message | Python client library | Google Cloud
official_sources.used_for: Message properties (data bytes, attributes map, message_id, ack_id, size, delivery_attempt None without a DeadLetterPolicy) and method semantics: ack() is best-effort unless exactly-once is enabled so processing must be idempotent; nack() causes redelivery 'immediately or after a delay... at this subscriber or another'; modify_ack_deadline(seconds) 'should be between 0 and 600. Due to network latency, values below 10 are advised against'; the class is not constructed directly by user code

official_sources.title: Manage message leases | Pub/Sub Documentation | Google Cloud
official_sources.used_for: client libraries extending the acknowledgment deadline up to an hour via periodic modifyAckDeadline; the three lease-management knobs (maximum extension period, maximum and minimum duration per extension) and their redelivery implications

official_sources.title: Method: projects.subscriptions.modifyAckDeadline | Pub/Sub | Google Cloud
official_sources.used_for: the hard service bounds behind lease extension: ackDeadlineSeconds minimum 0, maximum 600 seconds (10 minutes), and zero making the message immediately available for redelivery to another subscriber

research.language_rationale: google-cloud-pubsub is Google's official Python Cloud Client Library and the catalog item names it explicitly; the streaming-pull subscriber semantics under test (FlowControl, lease management, ack/nack/modack dispatch, StreamingPullFuture shutdown) exist only in that library, so the seed pins google-cloud-pubsub==2.39.0 (current release) via requirements.txt under the sanctioned dependency-seed mechanism and tests through the library's own injectable surface — real Message objects over a request queue and a subscribe()-compatible fake — rather than a toy re-implementation.
